description: Nepali phone number and numeric identifier reading patterns for TTS text normalization. Covers how different types of numbers (phone numbers, house numbers, license plates, PINs, bank accounts, years, flight numbers, scores) are read aloud in Nepali, including multiple valid reading styles with naturalness ratings.

notes.usage: Used by the TTS normalizer to convert numeric identifiers into their most natural spoken Nepali forms. The normalizer should detect the number type from context and apply the preferred reading pattern.
notes.no_fixed_convention: There is no single fixed convention for reading phone numbers in Nepali. Three patterns exist: (1) full number form, (2) digit-by-digit, (3) chunked as pairs/threes/fours. The most natural choice depends on the number type and context.
notes.digit_by_digit_default: For phone numbers and most long numeric identifiers, digit-by-digit reading with natural pause groupings is the most common pattern in spoken Nepali.
notes.zero_pronunciation: When reading digits aloud (phone numbers, PINs, etc.), सुन्ना is more common than शून्य in colloquial speech. शून्य is reserved for formal/mathematical contexts.
notes.pause_grouping: In digit-by-digit reading, speakers naturally insert micro-pauses to chunk digits. For mobile numbers, the typical grouping follows the written format: 98XX XXXXXXX or 98XX-XXX-XXXX.

reading_patterns.nepali: नौ आठ चार एक, पाँच दुई तीन चार पाँच छ
reading_patterns.romanization: nau āṭh cār ek, pā̃c duī tīn cār pā̃c cha
reading_patterns.description: Digit-by-digit with a pause after the first four digits matching the written grouping. This is how most Nepali speakers naturally read mobile numbers aloud.

reading_patterns.nepali: नौ आठ चार एक पाँच दुई तीन चार पाँच छ
reading_patterns.romanization: nau āṭh cār ek pā̃c duī tīn cār pā̃c cha
reading_patterns.description: Digit-by-digit without clear grouping pauses. Common when reading quickly.
Reading as two-digit pairs (98-41-52-34-56). This is unnatural for phone numbers in Nepali and would cause confusion.

reading_patterns.nepali: नौ अर्ब चौरासी करोड पन्ध्र लाख तेइस हजार चार सय छपन्न
reading_patterns.romanization: nau arba caurāsī karoḍ pandhr lākh teīs hajār cār say chapanna
reading_patterns.description: Reading as a single large number. Nobody reads phone numbers this way in Nepali.

phone_number_formats.description: Nepal mobile numbers start with 98 (NTC) or 97 (Ncell). Ten digits total. Always read digit-by-digit with natural pause grouping.

reading_patterns.nepali: सुन्ना एक, चार दुई तीन चार पाँच छ सात
reading_patterns.romanization: sunnā ek, cār duī tīn cār pā̃c cha sāt
reading_patterns.description: Area code read digit-by-digit, pause, then remaining digits read individually. The dash in writing corresponds to a natural pause.

reading_patterns.nepali: सुन्ना एक चार दुई तीन चार पाँच छ सात
reading_patterns.romanization: sunnā ek cār duī tīn cār pā̃c cha sāt
reading_patterns.description: All digits read sequentially without a strong pause at the dash.

reading_patterns.nepali: एक, चार दुई तीन चार पाँच छ सात
reading_patterns.romanization: ek, cār duī tīn cār pā̃c cha sāt
reading_patterns.description: Area code 01 spoken as just 'एक' (dropping the leading zero), then digits. Common in casual speech within the Kathmandu valley where the area code is assumed.

phone_number_formats.description: Kathmandu valley landline numbers use area code 01 followed by 7 digits. The leading zero of the area code is sometimes dropped in casual speech.

reading_patterns.nepali: सुन्ना दुई एक, पाँच दुई तीन चार पाँच छ
reading_patterns.romanization: sunnā duī ek, pā̃c duī tīn cār pā̃c cha
reading_patterns.description: Area code digits read individually, pause at the dash, then local number digits individually.

phone_number_formats.description: Landlines outside Kathmandu valley use 3-digit area codes (e.g., 021 for Biratnagar, 061 for Pokhara) followed by 6 digits.

reading_patterns.description: Read digit-by-digit. Short numbers are almost always read this way.
Reading as a full number is not natural for toll-free/service codes.

phone_number_formats.description: Nepal Telecom toll-free and service numbers. Always read digit-by-digit.
Emergency numbers are always read digit-by-digit. These are well-known short codes.

other_number_contexts.description: House numbers and room numbers are typically read as full numbers. कोठा नं. ३०१ is read as 'कोठा नम्बर तीन सय एक'. For larger numbers like ११०५, it would be 'एघार सय पाँच'.
Four-digit house/room numbers are also read as full numbers. घर नं. ११०५ is 'घर नम्बर एघार सय पाँच'.

other_number_contexts.spoken_nepali: बा एक च बाह्र सय चौँतीस
other_number_contexts.romanization: bā ek ca bāhr say caũtīs
other_number_contexts.description: Nepal license plates have a zone code (letters), a class digit, a series letter, and a 4-digit number. The zone and series letters are read as letters (बा, च), the class number as a digit, and the 4-digit number as a full number. बा = बागमती zone.

other_number_contexts.spoken_nepali: बा चौध प छपन्न सय अठहत्तर
other_number_contexts.romanization: bā caudh pa chapanna say aṭhhattar
other_number_contexts.description: Newer plates may have a 2-digit class number. The class number is read as a full number, as is the final 4-digit sequence.
PIN codes and postal codes are typically read digit-by-digit to ensure accuracy. Note the use of सुन्ना for zero rather than शून्य.

other_number_contexts.spoken_nepali: सुन्ना एक तीन सुन्ना आठ सात छ पाँच चार तीन दुई एक
other_number_contexts.romanization: sunnā ek tīn sunnā āṭh sāt cha pā̃c cār tīn duī ek
other_number_contexts.description: Bank account numbers are always read digit-by-digit for accuracy. Long digit sequences are never read as full numbers or paired. Speakers may naturally pause every 3-4 digits.
Bikram Sambat (BS) year numbers are read as full numbers. साल २०८१ is 'साल दुई हजार एकासी'. This is the standard and most natural reading.
In casual speech, BS years are sometimes shortened by splitting into two 2-digit numbers: २०८१ becomes 'बीस एकासी' (twenty eighty-one). This is informal but widely understood.
AD year numbers in Nepali context are read as full numbers. Shortened form (बीस पच्चीस) is also used informally.

Flight numbers have the airline code read as individual English letters and the numeric part read as a full number. RA = Nepal Airlines (Royal Nepal). The number part (205) is read as 'दुई सय पाँच', not digit-by-digit.
Other airline codes follow the same pattern. U4 = Buddha Air. The digit in the airline code is read as English, the flight number as a Nepali full number.
Sports scores and match results are read as individual numbers. The dash/hyphen is not spoken explicitly; instead a brief pause separates the two numbers. ३-१ is 'तीन एक' (not 'तीन माइनस एक').
In more formal sports commentary, the separator may be spoken as विरुद्ध (versus/against). '३-१ ले जित्यो' → 'तीन एक ले जित्यो' or 'तीन विरुद्ध एक ले जित्यो'.
Percentages are read as full numbers. The decimal point is दशमलव and the percent sign is प्रतिशत.
Currency amounts are always read as full numbers using the South Asian numbering system (lakh, crore). The unit रुपैयाँ typically comes after the number.
Municipal ward numbers are read as full numbers. 'वडा नं.' is expanded to 'वडा नम्बर'.
Bus route numbers are read as full numbers. Short identifiers (1-3 digits) are naturally read as complete numbers rather than digit-by-digit.

other_number_contexts.spoken_nepali: पैंसट्ठी सुन्ना एक पचहत्तर एक दुई तीन चार पाँच
other_number_contexts.romanization: paisaṭṭhī sunnā ek pachattar ek duī tīn cār pā̃c
other_number_contexts.description: Citizenship numbers are read in groups separated by dashes. Each group may be read as a full number (for short groups) or digit-by-digit (for longer groups). The dashes correspond to natural pauses.

digit_pronunciation_reference.usage_note: सुन्ना is strongly preferred when reading digits aloud in phone numbers, PINs, account numbers, etc. शून्य is used in formal/mathematical contexts (e.g., 'शून्य डिग्री सेल्सियस').
No formal/colloquial distinction for this digit.
No formal/colloquial distinction for this digit.
No formal/colloquial distinction for this digit.
No formal/colloquial distinction for this digit.
No formal/colloquial distinction for this digit.
No formal/colloquial distinction for this digit.
No formal/colloquial distinction for this digit.
No formal/colloquial distinction for this digit.

reading_style_decision_rules.condition: Number is identified as a phone number (mobile, landline, toll-free, emergency)
reading_style_decision_rules.description: Phone numbers are always read digit-by-digit. Group pauses follow the written format (dash positions).

reading_style_decision_rules.condition: Number is 1-4 digits and represents a label (room, ward, route, etc.)
reading_style_decision_rules.description: Short identifying numbers (house numbers, ward numbers, route numbers) are read as full numbers.

reading_style_decision_rules.condition: Number is 5+ digits and represents an identifier (account number, PIN, etc.)
reading_style_decision_rules.description: Long identifying numbers where each digit matters are read digit-by-digit for accuracy.

reading_style_decision_rules.condition: Number is identified as a year (BS or AD)
reading_style_decision_rules.description: Year numbers are read as full numbers. Shortened form (split into two 2-digit numbers) is acceptable in casual speech.

reading_style_decision_rules.condition: Number represents a quantity, amount, or measurement
reading_style_decision_rules.description: Quantities, currency amounts, and measurements are always read as full numbers using the South Asian numbering system.

reading_style_decision_rules.condition: Number is part of a vehicle license plate
reading_style_decision_rules.description: License plates use mixed reading: letters as letters, and numeric portions as full numbers.

reading_style_decision_rules.condition: Number pair represents a score or result (N-N format)
reading_style_decision_rules.description: Scores are read as individual numbers with a pause (or विरुद्ध in formal speech) replacing the dash.

reading_style_decision_rules.condition: Number contains dashes or dots as structural separators (e.g., citizenship number, date)
reading_style_decision_rules.description: Numbers with explicit separators are read group-by-group, with each group read as a full number (if short) or digit-by-digit (if long).
